EQP-1: Provides a Mode set with two values {on, off}.
EQP-2: Provides an integer threshold used by calibration and working conditions.
FUN-1: The system supports calibration: when Mode=on, the current value changes gradually and converges toward the desired value; when Mode=off, the value remains unchanged.
FUN-2: Calibration timing: initializes with clock t=0 and state s ∈ {on, off}.
FUN-3: The system models Peterson’s mutual exclusion algorithm with two processes. Each process may request access to the critical section. Boolean variables `req1`, `req2` are used to capture requests and scheduling.
FUN-4: The system provides a bounded buffer of capacity 3 with a logical reader/writer interface. Counters track the number of elements written and read, ensuring valid operations.
SAF-1: Mutual exclusion: Peterson’s algorithm guarantees that at most one process is in the critical section at any time.
SAF-2: Progress: if a process requests entry (`req1=true` or `req2=true`), it will eventually be granted access.
SAF-3: Buffer safety: counters are natural numbers, and the number of stored elements never exceeds the buffer capacity.

FUN-1: The model represents an island connected to the mainland by a one-way bridge.
FUN-2: The state consists of cars on the island, cars on the bridge in the direction of the island, and cars on the bridge in the direction of the mainland.
FUN-3: The system initializes with no cars on the island, bridge, or mainland.
EQP-1: The environment defines a finite set of cars (CARS).
EQP-2: The environment specifies a maximum bridge capacity, which bounds the number of cars in the system.
SAF-1: The sets of cars are finite.
SAF-2: The total number of cars does not exceed the bridge capacity.
SAF-3: Cars cannot simultaneously occupy both the island and the mainland (one-way constraint).

FUN-1: The model defines a clock operating in a 12-hour format with a meridian indicator (AM or PM).
FUN-2: At system start, the clock is initialized to 12:00 AM.
EQP-1: The Meridian Set consists of two constants representing the possible values of the meridian: AM and PM.
SAF-1: The clock format is always correct: the hour is between 1 and 12, the minutes are between 0 and 59, and the meridian is either AM or PM.
SAF-2: When the hour passes from 11:59 to 12:00, the meridian switches between AM and PM, ensuring that time progression converges correctly to the next half-day cycle.

FUN-1: The model describes the Bakery algorithm for mutual exclusion between processes.
EQP-1: Processes are represented as a set, and each process has a unique identifier given by a function Id.
EQP-2: The maximum cycle count is a natural number that limits how many actions a process may perform.
FUN-2: Each process may perform the following actions: request to start execution, request a ticket, enter the critical section if conditions allow, leave the critical section, or fail and stop execution.
FUN-3: Ticket assignment follows the Bakery principle: a process receives a strictly positive ticket number greater than all currently active tickets.
SAF-1: At any given time, at most one process is in the critical section, ensuring mutual exclusion.
SAF-2: Any process in the critical section must hold a valid ticket number that is non-zero.
SAF-3: If two processes both request entry, the one with the smaller ticket number will be granted entry first; if ticket numbers are equal, the one with the smaller Id enters first.

FUN-1: The system concerns people and locations.
FUN-2: People are permanently assigned the authorization to access certain locations.
FUN-3: A person which is in a location must be authorized to be there.
FUN-4: Turnstiles are normally blocked.
FUN-5: A person willing to pass through a turnsile puts its card in the fence of the card reader.
FUN-6: If the person is accepted, the green light is lit and the turnstile is unblocked for at most 30 seconds.
FUN-7: If the person is not accepted, the red light is lit for 2 seconds and the turnstile remains blocked.
FUN-8: As soon as an accepted person has gone through an unblocked turnstile, the green light is turned off and the turnstile is blocked again.
FUN-9: If nobody goes through an unblocked turnstile during the 30 seconds period, the green light is turned off and the turnstile is blocked again.
EQP-1: Each person receives a personal magnetic card.
EQP-2: Each entrance and exit of a location is equipped with a card reader.
EQP-3: Each card reader has two lights: one green light and one red light.
EQP-6: A sensor detects the passage of a person through a turnstile.
SAF-1: No persons must remain blocked in a location.
SAF-2: Any person authorized to be in a location must also be authorized to go in another location which communicates with the first one.
SAF-3: Any person authorized to be in a location which is not “outside”, must also be authorized to be in another location communicating with the former and leading towards outside.

EQP-1: The system has got the following pieces of equipment: a Motor, a Clutch, and a Door.
EQP-2: Four Buttons are used to start and stop the motor, and engage and disengage the clutch.
EQP-3: A Controller is supposed to manage these equipment.
FUN-1: Buttons and Controller are weakly synchronized.
FUN-2: Controller and Equipment are strongly synchronized
FUN-3: When the clutch is disengaged, the door cannot be closed several times, ONLY ONCE.
FUN-4: When the door is closed, the clutch cannot be disengaged several times, ONLY ONCE.
FUN-5: Opening and closing the door is not independent. It must be synchronized with disengaging and engaging the clutch.
SAF-1: When the clutch is engaged, the motor must work.
SAF-2: When the clutch is engaged, the door must be closed.

FUN-1: The system is controlling cars on a bridge connecting the mainland to an island.
FUN-2: The number of cars on bridge and island is limited.
FUN-3: The bridge is one-way or the other, not both at the same time.
EQP-1: The system is equipped with two traffic lights with two colors: green and red.
EQP-2: The traffic lights control the entrance to the bridge at both ends of it.
EQP-3: Cars are not supposed to pass on a red traffic light, only on a green one.
EQP-4: The system is equipped with four sensors with two states: on or off.
EQP-5: The sensors are used to detect the presence of a car entering or leaving the bridge: “on” means that a car is willing to enter the bridge or to leave it.

FUN-1: The protocol ensures the copy of a file from one site to another one.
FUN-2: The file is supposed to be made of a sequence of items.
FUN-3: The file is send piece by piece between the two sites.

ENV-1: We have a set of nodes forming a tree.
FUN-1: Each node has a counter, which is a natural number.
FUN-2: The difference between any two counters is at most equal to 1.
FUN-3: Each node can only read the counter of its immediate neighbors in the tree.
FUN-4: The counter of a node can be modified by this node only.

FUN-1: The Bounded Retransmission Protocol is a file transfer protocol. Its goal is to totally or partially transfer a certain non-empty original sequential file from one site, the sender, to another, the receiver.
FUN-2: A "total transfer" means that the transmitted file is an exact copy of the original one.
FUN-3: A "partial transfer" means that the transmitted file is a prefix of the original one.
FUN-4: Each site may end up in any of the two situations: either it believes that the protocol has terminated successfully , or it believes that the protocol has aborted before being successfully terminated.
FUN-5: When the sender believes that the protocol has terminated successfully then the receiver believes so too. Conversely, when the receiver believes that the protocol has aborted then the sender believes so too.
FUN-6: However, it is possible for the sender to believe that the protocol has aborted while the receiver believes that it has terminated successfully.
FUN-7: When the receiver believes that the protocol has terminated successfully, this is because the original file has been entirely copied on the receiver’s site. In other words, the receiver’s belief is true.
FUN-8: When the receiver believes that the protocol has aborted, this is because the original file has not been copied entirely on the receiver’s site. Again, the receiver’s belief is true.

EQP-1: Provides a function f defined on a finite domain and a value v to be searched.
EQP-2: The domain of f is assumed to be well-defined and finite.
FUN-1: The system searches for an index i such that f(i) = v.
FUN-2: In the abstract model m0, the final condition requires that i belongs to dom(f) and f(i) = v.
FUN-3: In the refinement m1, the system introduces a Boolean variable searching that indicates whether the search is ongoing. When searching is true, the procedure explores indices starting from n−1 downward.
FUN-4: Initialization sets i := n−1 to establish loop invariants.
FUN-5: If f(i) ≠ v, then the algorithm decrements i and continues.
FUN-6: A Boolean variable found represents whether the target has been located. When found becomes true, it must hold that f(i) = v and searching becomes false.
SAF-1: During the search, if searching is true, the condition v ∈ f[0..i] must always hold, ensuring that the target value remains within the considered subdomain.
SAF-2: At all times, the condition i ∈ dom(f) must hold. This ensures the algorithm never discards potential indices incorrectly.
SAF-3: When found is true, the property f(i) = v implies that the abstract requirement i ∈ dom(f) ∧ f(i) = v is satisfied.
DEG-1: If v is not present in the function image f[dom(f)], then found never becomes true and the search cannot succeed.
DEG-2: If the function f is undefined on some indices, the invariant i ∈ dom(f) may be violated.

EQP-1: Provides a natural number n as the input.
FUN-1: The system computes the integer square root of n, producing a value r such that r*r ≤ n < (r+1)*(r+1).
FUN-2: In the abstract model, r is chosen nondeterministically as a natural number satisfying the square root condition.
FUN-3: In the refined model, the algorithm initializes r = 0. A Boolean variable progressing indicates that the computation is ongoing. While progressing is true, r is incremented step by step until the square root condition becomes true.
SAF-1: At all times, r remains a natural number.
SAF-2: The invariant ensures r*r ≤ n during computation.
SAF-3: A Boolean variable done represents that the algorithm has terminated. When done is true, the property r*r ≤ n < (r+1)*(r+1) must hold.
DEG-1: If n < 0, the computation is undefined since no natural number square root exists.

EQP-1: The system provides core elements for pointer and mapping management: integer variable r, integer bijection g, pointer variables q and p, pointer mapping next, conversion function h, base mapping f, and fixed integer parameter n.
EQP-2: Valid ranges are defined: r is an integer, g is an integer bijection, q and p are linked to r via h or directly, and next is consistent with g to ensure logical coherence.
FUN-1: Boolean variables are introduced: fs indicates mapping is in progress, fn indicates mapping has completed successfully, and nf indicates abnormal termination. At any point, exactly one holds true.
FUN-2: Initialization of r: in the base model, r is assigned arbitrarily; in refined models, r is explicitly set to 1. After initialization, fs = true.
FUN-3: Initialization of g: in the base model, g is arbitrary; in refined models, g is constrained by f.
FUN-4: Initialization of pointers and next: in models with h, q=h(1), p=h(0), and next is linked to g via h; in simplified models, q=1, p=0, and next is bound directly to g.
FUN-5: Progression of r: while fs = true and r≠n, r is incremented by 1 and g(r) is updated to r−1, gradually transforming g toward the target mapping.
FUN-6: Pointer movement: while fs = true and q≠h(n), q is updated to next(q), p is set to the new q, and next(q) is reset to p.
FUN-7: Base model final state: fn = true when g maps 1..n−1 to 0..n−2 with g(i)=i−1 for all i∈1..n−1 and g=f⁻¹.
FUN-8: Refined model final state: fn = true when r=n or q=h(n) or q=n, meaning all mappings and pointer moves are complete.
FUN-9: Mapping conversion: in models with h, h links progress and pointer operations, converting r to q and r−1 to p, while transforming g’s integer mapping into next’s pointer mapping.
SAF-1: r is restricted to 1..n in refined models to prevent out-of-bound progress.
SAF-2: g’s mapping is restricted to 1..n−1 → 0..n, avoiding invalid or conflicting entries.
SAF-3: Pointer consistency is maintained: p is always the predecessor of q.
SAF-4: In the base model, g must equal f⁻¹ to preserve consistency with the base mapping rule.
SAF-5: In refined models, g(i)=i−1 must hold for all i∈1..n−1, ensuring the mapping is corrected step by step.
SAF-6: While fs = true, progress operations maintain invariants that guarantee eventual convergence to fn = true.
DEG-1: If r=n or q=h(n)/n, fs becomes false and fn becomes true, halting further operations and preventing infinite loops.
DEG-2: If next(q) is unavailable, fs becomes false and nf becomes true, preventing q from pointing to an illegal address.
DEG-3: If r is initialized outside 1..n in refined models, it is reset to r=1 and fs continues, ensuring a legal starting point.

EQP-1: Provides a finite natural number function f defined over the domain {0..n−1}.
EQP-2: Provides n as the size of the function’s domain.
FUN-1: The system must determine an index p such that f(p) = max(ran(f)).
FUN-2: In the abstract model maxi_m0, the final condition requires p ∈ dom(f) and f(p) = max(ran(f)).
FUN-3: In the refined model maxi_m1, the system introduces a Boolean variable searching to indicate that the maximum has not yet been located. The search interval is delimited by two indices p and q.
FUN-4: Initialization sets p = 0 and q = n−1, covering the entire domain and starting the search with searching = true.
FUN-5: If searching is true, and p < q with f(p) < f(q), the algorithm increments p to shrink the interval from the left.
FUN-6: If searching is true, and p < q with f(p) ≥ f(q), the algorithm decrements q to shrink the interval from the right.
FUN-7: When p ≥ q, searching becomes false, marking that the maximum index has been found.
SAF-1: As long as searching is true, the maximum of f is always contained within the current interval [p..q].
SAF-2: The interval [p..q] is never empty until termination, guaranteeing the maximum is not lost.
SAF-3: The final guard p≥q implies the abstract condition that p is the index of the maximum.
DEG-1: If n=0 (empty domain), no maximum can be defined and the algorithm cannot proceed.
DEG-2: If n=1, initialization directly satisfies the final condition (p=q=0).

EQP-1: Provides a finite function f: 0..n−1 → ℕ.
EQP-2: Provides n, the size of the sequence to reverse.
FUN-1: The system must reverse the function f, i.e., produce g such that g(i)=f(n−i−1) for all i∈0..n−1.
FUN-2: Abstract machine rev_m0 specifies this reversal as the guard condition of event final.
FUN-3: Machine rev_m1 refines rev_m0 by introducing a Boolean variable fr to indicate whether the sequence reversal is still in progress. It also uses indices i and j to implement a step-by-step reversal of the sequence.
FUN-4: Initialization sets g = f (copying the original sequence to g), i = 0, j = n−1, and fr = true. While reversing is true, at each step, swap the values of g(i) and g(j), then increment i and decrement j to move indices symmetrically inward.
FUN-5: When j ≤ i, fr becomes false, marking the end of the sequence reversal process; at this point, the sequence g is completely reversed relative to the original function f.
SAF-1: Invariant ensures i and j always remain in 0..n−1.
SAF-2: Invariant i+j = n−1 guarantees that indices move symmetrically inward.
SAF-3: Invariant partition: g[0..i−1] and g[j+1..n−1] are already reversed, g[i..j] still equals the original segment of f.
DEG-1: If n=0, g is empty, the final condition holds trivially.
DEG-2: If n=1, initialization directly sets i=0 and j=0, which immediately makes j ≤ i; fr becomes false, and g remains equal to f (no swap is needed as a single-element sequence is its own reverse).
DEG-3: If n=2, initialization sets i=0 and j=1. While fr is true, only one swap between g(0) and g(1) is performed; after swapping, i increments to 1 and j decrements to 0, making j ≤ i, fr becomes false, and the sequence reversal is completed.

EQP-1: Provides a finite array f: 0..n−1 ↣ ℕ.
EQP-2: Provides n, the size of the array, and a pivot value x.
FUN-1: The system must partition f into g such that all elements g(0..k) ≤ x and all elements g(k+1..n−1) > x.
FUN-2: p_m0 specifies this partition property only in the guards of event final.
FUN-3: p_m1 refines p_m0 by introducing a Boolean variable fp to indicate whether the array partitioning is still in progress. It also uses indices k and j to implement step-by-step partitioning.
FUN-4: Initialization: k = −1, g = f, j = −1, and fp = true.
FUN-5: While fp is true, scan elements with j from left to right. If g(j+1) > x, just increase j. If g(j+1) ≤ x, increment k and swap g(j+1) with g(k+1).
FUN-6: When j = n−1, fp becomes false, marking that the partitioning is complete and the final event holds.
SAF-1: Invariant ensures g is always a permutation of f (ran(g) = ran(f)).
SAF-2: For indices ≤ k, all values are ≤ x (left partition).
SAF-3: For indices k+1..j, all values are > x (middle scanned partition).
SAF-4: For indices beyond j, elements are still unprocessed, ensuring safety of partitioning step.
SAF-5: Invariant j ∈ −1..n−1 and k ∈ −1..j ensures indices stay in valid bounds.
DEG-1: If n=0, then trivially g=f and j = −1 = n−1, so fp becomes false immediately, and the final condition holds without any processing.
DEG-2: If all elements > x, then k remains = −1 and j simply increments until n−1, at which point fp becomes false, completing the partition with an empty left part.
DEG-3: If all elements ≤ x, then k increments with each step, and g remains f but shifted by swaps; when j reaches n−1, fp becomes false, with the left part including all elements.
DEG-4: Edge case: if pivot x is smaller than every element, partition gives empty left part (k = −1). If pivot is larger than all, partition gives full left part (k = n−1).

EQP-1: Provides a finite natural number function f defined on the domain {0..n−1}.
EQP-2: Provides n as the size of the domain of f.
FUN-1: The system must determine an index j such that f(j) = min(ran(f)).
FUN-2: In the abstract model m0, the final condition requires j ∈ dom(f) and f(j) = min(ran(f)).
FUN-3: In the refined model m1, the system introduces a Boolean variable searching to indicate whether the minimum has been fully identified. Two indices are maintained: i as the scanning index and j as the current candidate for the minimum.
FUN-4: Initialization sets i = 1 and j = 0, with searching = true.
FUN-5: While searching is true, if i ≠ n and f(i) < f(j), then update j := i and increment i.
FUN-6: While searching is true, if i ≠ n and f(i) ≥ f(j), then keep j unchanged and increment i.
FUN-7: When i = n, searching becomes false, marking that the index j now indicates the minimum value.
SAF-1: During execution, j always points to the position of the minimum among the explored prefix f[0..i−1].
SAF-2: At termination i=n, j is the index of the global minimum value.
SAF-3: All updates maintain the invariant, ensuring the algorithm never loses the true minimum during iteration.
DEG-1: If n=0, the domain is empty and no minimum can be defined.
DEG-2: If n=1, initialization (i=1, j=0) directly satisfies the final condition without iteration.

EQP-1: Provides a finite natural number function f defined on the domain {0..n−1}.
EQP-2: Provides n as the size of the domain of f.
FUN-1: The system must determine an index p such that f(p) = min(ran(f)).
FUN-2: In the abstract model m0, the final condition requires p ∈ dom(f) and f(p) = min(ran(f)).
FUN-3: In the refined model m1, the system introduces a Boolean variable narrowing to indicate whether the interval containing the minimum is still being reduced. The algorithm maintains an interval [p..q] that is guaranteed to contain the minimum value of f.
FUN-4: Initialization sets p = 0 and q = n−1 (covering the full domain of f), with narrowing = true.
FUN-5: While narrowing is true, if p < q and f(p) < f(q), then decrement q (discard the right boundary of the interval) to shrink the range containing the minimum.
FUN-6: While narrowing is true, if p < q and f(p) ≥ f(q), then increment p (discard the left boundary of the interval) to shrink the range containing the minimum.
FUN-7: When p ≥ q, narrowing becomes false, marking the end of interval reduction; at this point, the index p indicates the position of the minimum value of f.
SAF-1: Invariant ensures min(f[0..n−1]) always lies within f[p..q].
SAF-2: The interval [p..q] shrinks monotonically while preserving the true minimum.
SAF-3: At termination (when narrowing is false and p ≥ q), the unique index p is guaranteed to point to the global minimum value of f.
DEG-1: If n=0, the domain is empty and no minimum can be defined.
DEG-2: If n=1, initialization directly sets p=0 and q=0, which immediately makes p ≥ q; narrowing becomes false, and the final condition is satisfied without further interval reduction.

EQP-1: The environment provides two integers A and B as input.
EQP-2: It is assumed that a mathematical gcd function is defined for pairs of natural numbers.
FUN-1: Boolean variables are introduced: fs indicates the gcd computation is in progress, fn indicates successful completion, and nf indicates abnormal termination. At any point, exactly one holds true.
FUN-2: The system computes the greatest common divisor (GCD) of A and B.
FUN-3: In the abstract model m0, the condition fn = true requires that variable a equals gcd(A,B).
FUN-4: In the refined model m1, the algorithm implements Dijkstra’s subtraction-based method to compute gcd(A,B).
FUN-5: Initialization sets a := A and b := B, and fs := true.
FUN-6: While fs = true and a ≠ b: if a > b, then a is replaced by a−b; if b ≥ a, then b is replaced by b−a.
FUN-7: When a = b, fs becomes false and fn becomes true, yielding gcd(A,B) = a = b.
SAF-1: Variables a and b remain positive integers whenever fs = true.
SAF-2: At every step, gcd(a,b) remains equal to gcd(A,B), so correctness is preserved.
SAF-3: When fn = true, the result is a correct greatest common divisor of A and B.
DEG-1: If either A or B is zero or negative, fs becomes false and nf becomes true, indicating invalid input outside the intended domain.

EQP-1: Pprovides a finite natural number function f with domain {0..n−1}.
EQP-2: Defines n as the size of the function's domain.
FUN-1: The system must determine an index j such that f(j) = max(ran(f)).
FUN-2: In the abstract model maxi_m0, the final condition requires j ∈ dom(f) and f(j) = max(ran(f)).
FUN-3: In the refined model maxi_m1, the system introduces a Boolean variable searching to indicate whether the scan is still in progress. The search begins with j = 0 and i = 1.
FUN-4: If searching is true and the current value f(i) > f(j), then the algorithm updates j := i and increments i.
FUN-5: If searching is true and the current value f(i) ≤ f(j), then the algorithm keeps j unchanged and increments i.
FUN-6: When i reaches n, searching becomes false and a Boolean variable done is set to true, marking that the maximum index has been found.
SAF-1: As long as searching is true, the chosen index j always refers to the position of the maximum among the already scanned elements f[0..i−1].
SAF-2: The loop invariant guarantees correctness: once the loop ends, j points to the global maximum of f.
SAF-3: Proof obligations show that the concrete loop maintains the abstract post-condition from maxi_m0.
DEG-1: If n = 0 (empty domain), no maximum can be defined and the algorithm cannot proceed.
DEG-2: If f has only one element (n=1), the initialization already yields the maximum without entering the loop.

EQP-1: Provides a natural number n as input.
FUN-1: The system computes the integer square root of n by producing a result r such that r*r ≤ n < (r+1)*(r+1).
FUN-2: In the abstract model m0, r is chosen nondeterministically as a natural number satisfying the square root property.
FUN-3: In the refined model m1, the algorithm initializes r = 0, a = 1, b = 3. A Boolean variable progressing indicates that the computation is ongoing. While progressing is true, the system repeatedly updates r, a, and b.
FUN-4: Variable a tracks (r+1)^2, and variable b tracks the increment (2*r + 3) to update a efficiently without recomputing squares.
SAF-1: At all times, r remains a natural number.
SAF-2: The invariants ensure a = (r+1)^2 and b = 2*r+3, maintaining consistency between variables.
SAF-3: The invariant guarantees r*r ≤ n during the computation.
SAF-4: A Boolean variable done indicates that the system has terminated. When done is true, the result r must satisfy r*r ≤ n < (r+1)*(r+1).
DEG-1: If n < 0, the computation is undefined because no natural square root exists.

EQP-1: The system provides a finite natural number function f defined on domain 0..n−1.
EQP-2: The system provides n, the size of the sequence to be sorted.
FUN-1: The goal is to sort the elements of f into ascending order.
FUN-2: Boolean variables are introduced: fs indicates that sorting is in progress, fn indicates that the array has been successfully sorted, and nf indicates abnormal termination (e.g., if sorting cannot be completed). At any time, exactly one of these holds true.
FUN-3: In the abstract machine sr_m0, sorting is defined in the final condition: when fn = true, g is a bijection over 0..n−1 into ℕ, ran(g) = ran(f), and g is strictly increasing.
FUN-4: Machine sr_m1 refines sr_m0 by implementing selection sort. While fs = true, the algorithm finds the minimum element of the unsorted suffix g[k..n−1] and swaps it with g(k).
FUN-5: Variable k tracks the boundary between the sorted prefix [0..k−1] and the unsorted suffix [k..n−1]. The loop maintains fs = true until k = n−1.
FUN-6: Machine sr_m2 refines sr_m1 by detailing how the minimum is located in g[k..n−1], using variables l and j as scanning indices. While fs = true, invariants ensure that min(g[k..n−1]) is always in g[l..j].
FUN-7: When l = j, the minimum has been identified; it is swapped into position k, then k is incremented. If k = n−1 after increment, fs becomes false and fn becomes true.
SAF-1: While fs = true, the prefix g[0..k−1] is always sorted in strictly increasing order.
SAF-2: At all times, ran(g) = ran(f), ensuring no element is lost or duplicated during sorting.
SAF-3: During refinement sr_m2, invariants ensure that the candidate minimum is never lost during scanning: min(g[k..n−1]) ∈ g[l..j].
SAF-4: When fn = true, the entire array g is sorted in strictly increasing order.
SAF-5: If nf = true, then some error condition must have occurred, but the algorithm ensures fs and fn are never true simultaneously with nf.
DEG-1: If n = 0, the input is empty. fs immediately becomes false and fn is true; the final condition is trivially satisfied.
DEG-2: If n = 1, initialization sets k=0, and fs immediately becomes false with fn = true, since the array is already sorted.
DEG-3: If all elements of f are equal, every swap is redundant, but fs still eventually becomes false with fn = true, yielding a valid sorted array.

EQP-1: Provides two natural numbers p (dividend) and q (divisor) with q > 0.
FUN-1: Described an integer division model
FUN-2: A Boolean variable done is introduced to indicate termination. Once done is true, the quotient r must satisfy the condition r * q ≤ p and p < (r+1) * q.
FUN-3: The system initializes the quotient r to 0.
FUN-4: The system progresses by incrementing r while (r+1) * q ≤ p, ensuring convergence to the correct quotient.
SAF-1: At any point, r remains a natural number.
SAF-2: When the Boolean variable done is true, the result r must satisfy the division property: r * q ≤ p and p < (r+1) * q.
DEG-1: If q = 0, the division is undefined and the computation cannot proceed.

EQP-1: Provides a two-dimensional matrix f of size m×n.
EQP-2: Specifies a target value v to be searched within the matrix.
FUN-1: The system must determine a pair of indices (i, j) such that f(i, j) = v.
FUN-2: In the abstract model sm_m0, the final condition requires that i ∈ 0..m−1, j ∈ 0..n−1, and f(i, j) = v.
FUN-3: In the refinement sm_m1, the system introduces a Boolean variable searching that indicates whether the search is still in progress. When searching is true, the procedure begins at the bottom-right corner (i = m−1, j = n−1).
FUN-4: If searching is true and f(i, j) ≠ v with j = 0, then the algorithm updates i := i−1 and j := n−1, continuing the search in the previous row.
FUN-5: If searching is true and f(i, j) ≠ v with j ≠ 0, then the algorithm decrements j by 1 and continues searching.
FUN-6: The system introduces a Boolean variable found to represent whether the target value has been located. When found becomes true, it must hold that f(i, j) = v, and searching becomes false.
SAF-1: As long as searching is true, the value v, if present in the matrix, must remain within the unexplored submatrix [(0..i−1)×(0..n−1)] ∪ ({i}×(0..j)).
SAF-2: The algorithm ensures that no possible candidate position of v is skipped during the search.
SAF-3: When found is true, the condition f(i, j) = v guarantees that the abstract requirement of sm_m0 is satisfied.
DEG-1: If the value v is not present in the matrix, the algorithm will eventually exhaust all positions without success.
DEG-2: If m or n is 0 (empty matrix), the initialization cannot guarantee a valid starting index.

EQP-1: Provides two natural numbers p (dividend) and q (divisor) with q > 0.
FUN-1: Described an integer division model
FUN-2: The system initializes the quotient r as a natural number.
FUN-3: A Boolean variable done is introduced to represent termination. Once done is true, the condition r*q ≤ p and p < (r+1)*q must hold.
FUN-4: The system progresses by updating the quotient until the division property is satisfied.
FUN-5: In the refined model, the system initializes r = 0 and introduces an auxiliary variable a = q to track multiples of q.
FUN-6: Whenever a ≤ p, a Boolean flag step is set to true. In this case the system increments r by 1 and a by q, ensuring progress toward the correct quotient.
SAF-1: At any point, r remains a natural number.
SAF-2: Whenever step is true, it must hold that r*q ≤ p and a = (r+1)*q.
SAF-3: When done is true, the computed r must satisfy the integer division property: r*q ≤ p < (r+1)*q.
DEG-1: If q = 0, the division is undefined and the computation cannot proceed.

EQP-1: The system provides a finite increasing function f : ℕ → ℕ (or an array) of size n.
EQP-2: The system provides a natural number n as the search key.
FUN-1: The goal is to find an index r such that f(r) ≤ n < f(r+1).
FUN-2: Boolean variables are introduced: fs indicates the search is ongoing, fn indicates the target index has been found, and nf indicates the search ends with no valid index. At any time, exactly one of these holds true.
FUN-3: In the abstract model m0, the requirement is expressed in the guards of the final event: when fn = true, r must satisfy f(r) ≤ n < f(r+1).
FUN-4: Machine m1 introduces an interval [r..q] with fs = true, representing the current candidate range where the solution must lie.
FUN-5: Machine m1 defines two progress events: inc increases the lower bound r, and dec decreases the upper bound q, while keeping the condition f(r) ≤ n < f(q+1).
FUN-6: Machine m2 refines m1 by choosing the midpoint (r+1+q) ÷ 2 as the candidate index in both inc and dec, making the search strategy deterministic.
FUN-7: The final event occurs when fs becomes false. If fn = true, then r=q and f(r) ≤ n < f(r+1). If nf = true, the interval is exhausted without a valid index.
SAF-1: At all times when fs = true, r and q remain natural numbers with r ≤ q.
SAF-2: The interval invariant f(r) ≤ n < f(q+1) is preserved while fs = true, ensuring correctness of the shrinking interval.
SAF-3: This guarantees that the correct boundary index, if it exists, is never excluded during the search.
SAF-4: When fn = true, the algorithm ensures that r satisfies f(r) ≤ n < f(r+1).
SAF-5: When nf = true, it is guaranteed that no index satisfies the condition.
DEG-1: If n < f(0), the algorithm stabilizes with nf = true at r=0, q=0 (no valid index).
DEG-2: If n ≥ f(max), the algorithm stabilizes with nf = true at r=max, q=max (beyond range).
DEG-3: If n lies strictly between two consecutive values of f, the algorithm converges to the boundary with fn = true.
DEG-4: If n exactly equals f(k), the algorithm converges with fn = true and r=k.

EQP-1: The system provides a sorted finite function f : 0..n−1 → ℕ.
EQP-2: The system provides a search target value v ∈ ℕ.
EQP-3: The parameter n > 0 specifies the array size.
FUN-1: The goal is to find an index r such that f(r) = v, if such an index exists.
FUN-2: In the abstract model m0, r, p, q are abstract placeholders, and progress events are not specified.
FUN-3: Boolean variables are introduced: fs indicates the search is ongoing, fn indicates the target has been found, and nf indicates the target is not found (interval exhausted). Exactly one of these flags is true at any time.
FUN-4: In m1, the final condition requires r ∈ dom(f) and f(r) = v when fn = true.
FUN-5: In m2, the search interval [p..q] is introduced, with fs = true. The requirement v ∈ f[p..q] is preserved as long as fs = true.
FUN-6: In m2, if f(r) < v, then progress1 updates p := r+1 and chooses a new r within [p..q]; if f(r) ≥ v, then progress2 updates q := r−1 and chooses r within [p..q].
FUN-7: In m3, the algorithm becomes deterministic: when fs = true, r is always chosen as the midpoint of [p..q].
FUN-8: In m4, guards are simplified (¬f(r)<v instead of f(r)≥v) but the logic remains unchanged.
FUN-9: When f(r) = v, fs becomes false and fn = true, meaning the target is found at index r.
FUN-10: If the interval [p..q] becomes empty without success, fs becomes false and nf = true, meaning the search terminates without finding v.
SAF-1: During execution (fs = true), r always lies within the current bounds p..q.
SAF-2: The interval condition v ∈ f[p..q] ensures the target remains inside the current search range as long as fs = true.
SAF-3: progress1 guarantees that p increases safely (p = r+1 ≤ q).
SAF-4: progress2 guarantees that q decreases safely (p ≤ r−1).
SAF-5: When fn = true, the index r satisfies the abstract condition f(r) = v.
SAF-6: When nf = true, the interval is empty, and the algorithm ensures that v is not present in the function.
DEG-1: If v < f(0), the search shrinks to an empty interval, leading to nf = true.
DEG-2: If v > f(n−1), the same outcome occurs: nf = true.
DEG-3: If v = f(k) for some k, the algorithm converges to r = k with fn = true.
DEG-4: If multiple equal values exist, the algorithm may return any valid index r, depending on implementation choices.
